narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared a solid profile with a high family income bracket, an undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which is actually above the typical 3.21 average—an LSAT score of 35, and plans to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 60.5%, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. This prediction hinged most strongly on your academic decile in Year 3 of school, where you ranked in the 2nd decile—well below the average 5.53—which powerfully pushed the model toward forecasting bar failure by signaling weaker performance in later undergraduate years. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 3, also below the typical 5.71 average, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles and further elevating the failure risk. Your undergraduate GPA provided some counterbalance, as its above-average strength nudged the prediction slightly toward passing success, but it wasn't enough to offset the dominant impact of those lower school deciles. Compared to typical students, who tend to cluster in higher deciles and show steadier grade progress, your profile stood out for those early and mid-undergrad dips, which the model weighed heavily as bar exam predictors. While factors like your LSAT and full-time status aligned with averages and didn't sway things much, the Year 3 and Year 1 deciles were the standout drivers here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model focuses on patterns tied to first-time bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or targeted GPA improvement, and reapply with updated transcripts showing upward trends. You might also seek mentorship from Hispanic law associations for bar prep strategies that address these patterns. With focused effort on those academic foundations, your strong GPA and background could shine brighter next time. Keep pushing; rejections like this often fuel comebacks in law.
